item.title: Ivanti CVE-2026-10520 Exploitation, LangGraph RCE Chain, and Cybercrime Platform Takedowns
item.description: This digest window is dominated by two parallel themes: high-impact vulnerabilities in exposed enterprise and AI infrastructure, and major law-enforcement disruption of cybercrime service providers. Reporting also highlights a significant public-sector messaging breach affecting French government...
item.content:encoded:
This digest window is dominated by two parallel themes: high-impact vulnerabilities in exposed enterprise and AI infrastructure, and major law-enforcement disruption of cybercrime service providers. Reporting also highlights a significant public-sector messaging breach affecting French government users, underscoring continued risk around collaboration platforms and identity compromise. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to patch CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection vulnerability in Ivanti Sentry, within three days under Binding Operational Directive 26-04. The flaw affects Ivanti Sentry, formerly MobileIron Sentry, and was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog after confirmed exploitation in attacks. Shadowserver reported that attackers had already backdoored many internet-exposed Ivanti Sentry gateways and warned that unpatched systems are likely compromised. The directive reflects the conditions defined in BOD 26-04: public exposure, KEV listing, potential for automated exploitation at scale, and attacker ability to gain partial or total control of affected systems. Researchers also disclosed three now-patched vulnerabilities in LangGraph, including a critical exploit chain affecting self-hosted deployments. The chain combines CVE-2025-67644, a SQL injection flaw in the SQLite checkpoint implementation affecting langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite versions before 3.0.1, with CVE-2026-28277, an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue affecting langgraph versions before 1.0.10. In self-hosted environments using the SQLite checkpointer with user-controlled filter input, the issues can be chained to execute attacker-controlled code; Check Point said LangChain’s managed LangSmith Deployment platform is not affected by the chain. INTERPOL-led Operation Ramz disrupted the Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform between October 2025 and February 2026. Authorities from 13 Middle East and North Africa countries made 201 arrests, including Guedz, identified as the primary developer and administrator, and seized hardware containing phishing software and scripts. Sniper Dz provided ready-made phishing kits, hosting infrastructure, and operational support targeting brands including PayPal, Facebook, Instagram, Yahoo, Netflix, and Steam. Group-IB said the platform had collected more than 45,000 victim records and was associated with more than 20,000 unique domains, reflecting the industrialized scale of phishing enablement services. Europol also reported the disruption of AudiA6, a cryptocurrency laundering service allegedly used by ransomware gangs and other cybercriminal networks to launder more than €336 million since 2021. The June 10 operation included arrests of two alleged administrators in Georgia, searches, takedowns of 25 domains, seizure of more than 30 servers, and blocking of Telegram accounts used by the network. U.S. authorities charged Ruslan Igorevich Tkachuk and Alexander Vladimirovich Ledenev with money laundering-related offenses, while Europol said AudiA6 relied on thousands of fraudulent exchange accounts and more than 6,000 KYC records tied to money mule accounts. France’s interministerial digital directorate, DINUM, said a breach of the Tchap encrypted messaging platform may have affected 73,467 public-sector employees, representing less than 9% of registered users. The attacker accessed Tchap using a compromised user account, which DINUM said it identified and immediately blocked. DINUM stated that private Tchap conversations remain encrypted and protected, while public chat rooms are not encrypted and their shared data may have been accessed. Potentially exposed account data includes last name, first name, email address, public-sector entity, and avatar. A threat actor claimed responsibility for the breach and alleged they scraped nearly 650,000 messages, information from more than 73,000 accounts, and documents and media files shared through the platform. The incident highlights the exposure created when compromised user accounts intersect with semi-public collaboration spaces, even where private messaging remains encrypted.

CISA orders feds to patch actively exploited Ivanti flaw by Sunday — Must Read

CISA has ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch the actively exploited Ivanti Sentry flaw CVE-2026-10520 within three days under its new BOD 26-04 directive. Shadowserver warns that unpatched internet-exposed Sentry systems are likely already compromised due to widespread exploitation using a public proof-of-concept.

  • CISA ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to patch the actively exploited Ivanti Sentry vulnerability CVE-2026-10520 within three days under Binding Operational Directive 26-04.
  • CVE-2026-10520 is a maximum-severity OS command injection vulnerability in Ivanti Sentry, formerly known as MobileIron Sentry.
  • Shadowserver reported that attackers had already backdoored many internet-exposed Ivanti Sentry gateways and warned that unpatched systems are likely compromised.
  • CISA added CVE-2026-10520 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog after confirming active exploitation in attacks.
  • BOD 26-04 requires federal agencies to prioritize patching when assets are publicly exposed, flaws are listed in CISA’s KEV catalog, exploitation can be automated at scale, and exploitation gives attackers partial or total control of a system.

INTERPOL Operation Takes Down Sniper Dz Phishing Platform, Arrests Administrator — Should Read

INTERPOL’s Operation Ramz dismantled the Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform and led to 201 arrests across the MENA region, including its alleged administrator in Algeria. The decade-old service enabled large-scale phishing with ready-made kits and infrastructure tied to more than 20,000 domains and 45,000 victim records.

  • INTERPOL-led Operation Ramz disrupted the Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform between October 2025 and February 2026.
  • Authorities from 13 countries in the Middle East and North Africa region made 201 arrests, including Guedz, the primary developer and administrator of Sniper Dz.
  • Authorities took down the website used to offer Sniper Dz’s phishing-as-a-service capabilities and seized hardware containing phishing software and scripts.
  • Group-IB said Sniper Dz had collected more than 45,000 victim records and was associated with more than 20,000 unique domains.
  • Sniper Dz offered ready-made phishing kits, hosting infrastructure, and operational support, targeting major organizations such as PayPal, Facebook, Instagram, Yahoo, Netflix, and Steam.

LangGraph Flaw Chain Exposes Self-Hosted AI Agents to Remote Code Execution — Should Read

Researchers disclosed three patched LangGraph flaws, including a chain in self-hosted deployments that can combine SQL injection with unsafe deserialization to achieve remote code execution. Users running affected SQLite or Redis checkpointers should update immediately and restrict exposure of LangGraph servers and checkpoint access.

  • Cybersecurity researchers disclosed three now-patched vulnerabilities affecting LangGraph, including a critical chain that could lead to remote code execution.
  • CVE-2025-67644 is a SQL injection flaw in LangGraph's SQLite checkpoint implementation that affects langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite versions before 3.0.1.
  • CVE-2026-28277 is an unsafe msgpack deserialization flaw in LangGraph that affects langgraph versions before 1.0.10.
  • CVE-2025-67644 and CVE-2026-28277 can be chained in self-hosted deployments using the SQLite checkpointer with user-controlled filter input to execute attacker-controlled code.
  • Check Point said LangChain's managed LangSmith Deployment platform is not affected by the vulnerability chain.

Over 73,000 French govt employees affected in Tchap messenger breach — Should Read

A breach of France’s Tchap government messaging platform exposed public chat data tied to 73,467 public-sector employees, including names, email addresses, organizations, and avatars. DINUM says private encrypted conversations remain protected, but the attacker allegedly scraped messages and files from unencrypted public rooms after gaining access through a compromised account.

  • DINUM said a breach of the Tchap encrypted messaging platform may have affected 73,467 French public-sector employees, representing less than 9% of registered users.
  • The attacker gained access to Tchap using a compromised user account, which DINUM identified and immediately blocked.
  • DINUM said private conversations on Tchap remain encrypted and protected, but public chat rooms are not encrypted and their shared data may have been accessed.
  • Potentially exposed user account data includes last name, first name, email address, public-sector entity, and avatar.
  • A threat actor claimed responsibility for the breach and alleged they scraped nearly 650,000 messages and information from more than 73,000 accounts, as well as documents and media files shared through Tchap.

Europol Disrupts AudiA6 Crypto Laundering Service Used by Ransomware Gangs — Should Read

Europol and international partners disrupted AudiA6, a crypto laundering service allegedly used by ransomware gangs to wash more than €336 million since 2021. Two alleged administrators were arrested in Georgia, infrastructure was seized, and U.S. charges could carry up to 20 years in prison.

  • Europol said authorities disrupted AudiA6, a cryptocurrency laundering service used by ransomware gangs and cybercriminal networks to launder more than €336 million since 2021.
  • The June 10, 2026 operation included the arrest of two alleged administrators in Georgia, searches, takedowns of 25 domains, seizure of more than 30 servers, and blocking of Telegram accounts used by the network.
  • The U.S. Department of Justice charged Ruslan Igorevich Tkachuk and Alexander Vladimirovich Ledenev with conspiracy to launder monetary instruments and sting money laundering, each carrying a maximum possible sentence of 20 years in prison if convicted.
  • Europol said AudiA6 used thousands of fraudulent exchange accounts opened with stolen or purchased identities and identified more than 6,000 KYC records linked to money mule accounts.
  • AudiA6 allegedly operated as a cryptocurrency mixing service that returned “cleaned” funds within an hour through complex transaction chains while charging commissions between 3 percent and 10 percent.

item.title: CVE-2026-50751 Check Point VPN Bypass and Cloud DNS Takeover SEO Campaign
item.description: This coverage window is dominated by two concrete exposure patterns: trust boundary failures in internet-facing access infrastructure and abuse of abandoned cloud DNS delegations. The reporting highlights how legacy protocol code and neglected DNS assets can both enable attackers to inherit...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window is dominated by two concrete exposure patterns: trust boundary failures in internet-facing access infrastructure and abuse of abandoned cloud DNS delegations. The reporting highlights how legacy protocol code and neglected DNS assets can both enable attackers to inherit legitimacy from trusted enterprise environments. Check Point released hotfixes on June 8, 2026 for CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass rated CVSS 9.3 affecting Mobile Access/SSL VPN, Remote Access VPN, and Spark Firewall products. watchTowr Labs analyzed the issue and attributed it to deprecated IKEv1 VPN code, where attacker-controlled input in a VPNExtFeatures Vendor ID payload could influence authentication-related flags inside the gateway implementation. According to the research, the vulnerable implementation could be made to skip certificate signature validation during IKE authentication. watchTowr demonstrated that a vulnerable gateway accepted a random HASHI and created a phase-1 IKE security association, while a patched system rejected the same attempt with an authentication failure. The bypass reportedly worked across UDP 500 and TCP 443 Visitor Mode. The article states that an attacker could authenticate as a provisioned Remote Access user by presenting a matching subject distinguished name, a self-signed X.509 certificate, and an invalid signature, because the gateway did not verify the signature or certificate trust chain. watchTowr reported successful exploitation across Certificate, Certificate with enrollment, and Mixed user authentication modes, making the issue relevant to multiple Remote Access deployment configurations. Cyble Research & Intelligence Labs reported an SEO poisoning campaign that served Thai-language gambling content from trusted enterprise domains by exploiting abandoned or misconfigured DNS infrastructure. The campaign affected 163 organizations across more than 30 countries, with the primary technique involving Azure DNS zone takeover through reclaimed nameserver delegations tied to deleted Azure DNS zones. The campaign also used DigitalOcean DNS zone takeovers, direct wildcard DNS misconfigurations, and individually created A records in at least one Verizon subdomain environment. By inheriting authority over abandoned DNS delegations, the operators were able to publish gambling pages under otherwise legitimate organizational domains, increasing the apparent trust and search visibility of the content. Cyble described a consistent technical footprint across the gambling kit, including valid Let’s Encrypt wildcard TLS certificates, a recurring Next.js build fingerprint, Thai-language SEO content, and affiliate redirect codes such as ibiza99vip1, bigwinv1, link99, and seven77vip1. The infrastructure was linked to three OVH delivery nodes and a 103-node backend fleet in Hong Kong under AS398478, with shared indicators including a broker-xm.com certificate and an identical HTTP body hash.

item.title: CISA BOD 26-04, The Gentlemen RaaS, and Breach Reporting Integrity Issues
item.description: This coverage window was dominated by vulnerability management policy changes, ransomware infrastructure reporting, and data-handling failures affecting public trust in breach disclosures. The most consequential development for defenders is CISA’s BOD 26-04, which formalizes risk-based remediation...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window was dominated by vulnerability management policy changes, ransomware infrastructure reporting, and data-handling failures affecting public trust in breach disclosures. The most consequential development for defenders is CISA’s BOD 26-04, which formalizes risk-based remediation expectations for U.S. federal civilian agencies amid concern over slow patching and faster vulnerability weaponization. Separately, reporting on The Gentlemen ransomware ecosystem and abuse of public breach portals highlights how exposed infrastructure and weak verification processes continue to shape both operational risk and public incident narratives. CISA issued Binding Operational Directive 26-04 on June 10, 2026, replacing BOD 22-01 and BOD 19-02 with a risk-based vulnerability remediation framework for Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies. The directive evaluates vulnerabilities using four binary criteria: whether the affected asset is publicly exposed, whether the CVE appears in CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, whether exploitation is automatable by an adversary, and whether exploitation grants total or partial control of the system. The resulting 16 risk combinations map to remediation timelines ranging from three days with mandatory forensic triage for the highest-risk vulnerabilities to deferral until the next system upgrade when none of the risk criteria apply. CISA cited declining remediation effectiveness and AI-accelerated vulnerability discovery and weaponization as drivers for the shift, noting that the 2026 Verizon DBIR found only 26% of KEV-listed vulnerabilities were fully remediated by organizations in 2025. Agencies are required to update vulnerability management policies immediately, revise remediation processes within 60 days, and meet the full Table 1 remediation timelines within 180 days. This policy context overlaps with Silent Push reporting on The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service operation, where affiliates reportedly favor internet-facing VPN and firewall appliances for initial access. Silent Push described earlier infrastructure research around a malware loader it named CountLoader, seen in .NET, PowerShell, and JScript variants during July and August 2025, and linked activity to Cobalt Strike watermarks associated with affiliate activity spanning Black Basta, LockBit, Qilin, and The Gentlemen. Silent Push’s article contextualized recent reporting by Brian Krebs that identified the person allegedly behind The Gentlemen ransomware operation, while emphasizing Silent Push’s prior focus on infrastructure and affiliate behavior. The company reported that two Cobalt Strike watermarks, 1473793097 and 1357776117, were tied to a single ransomware affiliate connected to multiple ransomware ecosystems, including Black Basta, LockBit, Qilin, and The Gentlemen. One notable infrastructure overlap involved IP address 91.107.247[.]163, which Silent Push says it flagged as Cobalt Strike command-and-control on February 8, 2026. Check Point later identified the same IP in an April 20, 2026 DFIR report on a The Gentlemen intrusion, reinforcing the reported linkage between previously observed infrastructure and later incident-response findings. The reporting portrays The Gentlemen not as an isolated ransomware brand, but as part of a broader affiliate-driven ecosystem with reusable tooling and cross-family operational ties. Kyushu Electric Power Co., Inc. disclosed that a missing external storage device may affect private data tied to as many as 10.9 million customer accounts. The drive was reportedly used on April 27 for a backup because of server storage capacity constraints and was later found missing from a server room cabinet on May 26. Data on the device included customer names, service location addresses, electricity usage data, telephone numbers, retail electricity provider names, and related information, but the company said it did not include bank account or credit card data. The company reported the incident to Japan’s Personal Information Protection Commission and relevant authorities, and media reports said a police report was filed on June 4. Kyushu Electric Power also said impacted customers would be notified individually. The incident is significant because of the scale of potentially affected customer records and the involvement of utility-sector customer data, even without direct payment-card or bank-account exposure. Separately, Maine’s public breach notification portal was abused to publish fraudulent data breach disclosures before their legitimacy could be verified. VRChat told BleepingComputer that a breach notification posted in the Maine Attorney General’s database was fake, submitted under a fictitious employee name, and did not indicate compromised systems or data. A suspicious filing allegedly from Discord claimed 10 million affected individuals but included unreliable indicators such as a Gmail contact, placeholder phone number, and inconsistent dates. The Maine Office of the Attorney General confirmed that anyone can submit a breach notification form and have it added to the portal without verification. The reporting highlights how public breach repositories can become vectors for misinformation when submission and publication processes lack validation, potentially causing reputational harm and public confusion around real versus fabricated incidents.

item.title: ShinyHunters Exploits Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273 in University Data Theft Campaign
item.description: This digest window is dominated by reporting on active exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273, a critical unauthenticated remote code execution zero-day tied to data theft activity attributed to ShinyHunters. The reporting also reflects a broader concern that AI-assisted vulnerability...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by reporting on active exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273, a critical unauthenticated remote code execution zero-day tied to data theft activity attributed to ShinyHunters. The reporting also reflects a broader concern that AI-assisted vulnerability discovery may be compressing exploit timelines faster than traditional patch cycles can absorb. Google Mandiant attributed a PeopleSoft exploitation campaign to ShinyHunters, tracked as UNC6240, with observed activity dated between May 27 and June 9. The exploited vulnerability, CVE-2026-35273, affects Oracle PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools and carries a CVSS score of 9.8 due to unauthenticated remote code execution over HTTP without user interaction. The campaign appears heavily concentrated in higher education. Mandiant notified more than 100 organizations with vulnerable endpoints, and 68% were in higher education, mostly in the United States. The University of Nottingham confirmed a breach, and Have I Been Pwned counted approximately 455,000 unique email addresses in a leaked dataset associated with the incident. Oracle confirmed that CVE-2026-35273 affects PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools versions 8.61 and 8.62 and released emergency mitigations while indicating that a patch is forthcoming. BleepingComputer reported that ShinyHunters claimed to have used a “gadget chain” combining older flaws and zero-day vulnerabilities, allegedly stealing data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations. Researcher Michael R also identified exposed online directories containing attack-related tooling and shared IP addresses relevant to the campaign. Cisco Talos highlighted analysis from Yuri Kramarz arguing that AI-driven vulnerability discovery is outpacing human-centered patching processes. The article frames frontier AI models as capable of autonomously discovering and exploiting zero-days in minutes, compressing the traditional vulnerability lifecycle and increasing the operational pressure on defenders. The Talos discussion places the PeopleSoft activity in a broader context: organizations face a threat environment where exploit development, weaponization, and deployment may occur faster than conventional vulnerability management workflows can respond. The piece emphasizes foundational security controls, segmentation, behavioral detection, and validation exercises as part of a fallback model for environments where patch-first strategies may not keep pace with emerging exploitation timelines. Permiso announced that it won Best Identity Threat Detection and Response Platform at the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards, presented by The Hacker News. The item reflects continued industry focus on identity threat detection and response as a dedicated security category, particularly as credential misuse, cloud identity abuse, and post-compromise identity activity remain central to modern intrusion chains.

item.title: CalPhishing, The Gentlemen Ransomware, Miasma/Hades, and AI Agent OAuth Risk
item.description: This digest window is dominated by social engineering techniques moving beyond conventional email, expanding ransomware operations, and renewed concern over software supply chain exposure on developer systems. Reporting also highlights how AI agents and autonomous workflows are changing trust...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by social engineering techniques moving beyond conventional email, expanding ransomware operations, and renewed concern over software supply chain exposure on developer systems. Reporting also highlights how AI agents and autonomous workflows are changing trust boundaries around OAuth, prompt injection, and managed security operations. Health-ISAC reported an emerging healthcare-sector social engineering pattern called CalPhishing, in which attackers use iCalendar files and enterprise calendar auto-processing to place malicious links directly into employee schedules. Recent campaigns reportedly use ConsentFix, also described as device code phishing, to capture session tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication protections after the user interaction stage. Netskope Threat Labs framed OAuth authorization abuse as a structural scaling problem, particularly as organizations adopt AI agents that rely on persistent tokens, broad permissions, opaque runtimes, and autonomous workflows. The article described a confused deputy scenario in which an attacker plants malicious instructions in data consumed by an agent, causing the agent to use its own valid OAuth token to perform attacker-directed actions. SpecterOps published research on automating indirect prompt injection testing against an agentic system with access to Notion search, shell execution, and web-fetching tools. The workflow used poisoned Notion content to progress from simple echo payloads to full-chain payloads involving command execution and HTTP exfiltration, while noting that newer Claude Sonnet model behavior detected some earlier payload styles more reliably. StepSecurity reported that the Miasma worm and its PyPI branch Hades are spreading across npm and PyPI ecosystems, with execution paths tied to project opening, package installation, or package import. Miasma uses a small binding.gyp file in a technique described as “Phantom Gyp,” while Hades relies on an obfuscated import hook in init.py, creating visibility gaps for tools focused primarily on install-time checks, process behavior, or declared dependencies. Aikido highlighted npm v12, scheduled for July 2026, as a major package-manager security change because dependency install scripts will no longer run by default unless packages are allowlisted. The release is also expected to block Git dependencies and remote URL dependencies unless explicitly permitted, and to treat implicit node-gyp rebuilds triggered by binding.gyp as install scripts. Socket announced Andrew Becherer as its first Chief Information Security Officer, positioning the appointment around enterprise concerns with open source, AI tooling, and malicious package risk. The company said it now protects more than 27,000 organizations and framed the role as both an internal security function and a customer-facing software supply chain security leadership position. PRODAFT reporting cited by The Hacker News tracks The Gentlemen ransomware operation as Phantom Mantis and says the group shifted from affiliate activity into an independent partnership program in July 2025. The operation has claimed 478 victims, reportedly accounted for 10% of ransomware activity in April 2026, and includes Go-based ransomware that Microsoft tracks under Storm-2697 with a self-propagation mode when launched with the --spread argument. Law enforcement dismantled AudiA6, a cryptocurrency service Europol said was used by ransomware actors and other cybercriminals to launder more than $380 million. Authorities arrested two individuals in Georgia, seized domains, vehicles, properties, cryptocurrency assets, Telegram accounts, and retrieved 6,000 Know-Your-Customer records tied to alleged money mule accounts. In the U.K., Great Marlow School in Buckinghamshire sent most students home for a second consecutive day after a cybersecurity incident affected ICT systems, while students taking GCSE and A-Level exams were allowed onsite. The incident type was not confirmed, but the reporting cited nearly 2,000 education and childcare-sector cyber incidents recorded by the U.K. Information Commissioner’s Office between 2019 and 2025. Russian national Denis Obrezko appeared in U.S. federal court after transfer from Thailand, where he was arrested in November, over allegations that he supported the Russian state-linked Void Blizzard group. Prosecutors allege cryptocurrency transactions linked to Obrezko funded infrastructure used in attacks, and an FBI affidavit identified at least 11 compromised U.S. companies while suggesting the actual victim count is higher. In U.S. defense policy, a Senate Armed Services Committee amendment to create a standalone Cyber Force failed by a 14-13 vote. The debate reflected bipartisan frustration over cyber personnel readiness for U.S. Cyber Command, while the Senate bill separately proposed a new Under Secretary of Defense for Cyber, Information, and Networks, dual-hatted as the Pentagon CIO and principal cyber advisor.

item.title: ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, Coupang Fine, LangGraph CVEs, and AI Agent Risks
item.description: This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of...
item.content:encoded: This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of phishing and vishing tactics that continue to rely on trusted brands and user interaction. The University of Nottingham confirmed a cyber incident involving external access to a significant amount of data affecting current and former students. ShinyHunters claimed on its extortion site that it obtained more than 40GB of material, including payment-related data, while HaveIBeenPwned analysis of partially published data identified roughly 455,000 unique email addresses and associated personal, academic, and fee information. In South Korea, Coupang received a record 624.6 billion won, approximately $409 million, fine from the Personal Information Protection Commission after a breach affecting about 37.55 million people. The regulator cited authentication signature key management failures, access control deficiencies, data destruction and notification violations, and obstruction of the investigation; authorities identified a former Coupang IT employee, a 43-year-old Chinese national, as the primary suspect. Multiple reports emphasized that attackers are increasingly operating through identity systems rather than relying solely on traditional exploit chains. Flashpoint reported more than 11.1 million devices infected with infostealers last year, producing over 3.3 billion stolen credentials, cookies, cloud tokens, and other identity artifacts circulating in criminal markets. AI agent security emerged as a recurring concern across SaaS, developer workflows, and CI/CD environments. Varonis found that the OpenClaw email agent Pinchy could be tricked by ordinary-looking phishing emails into forwarding sensitive material, while Microsoft disclosed and Anthropic patched a Claude Code GitHub Action issue that could expose workflow secrets when AI agents processed untrusted GitHub content. AppOmni described agentic AI as a new class of SaaS identity capable of fetching data, creating users, and modifying configurations. Related identity exposure analysis from XM Cyber highlighted the risk of unmanaged human, machine, service, cloud, and AI accounts across hybrid environments, particularly where credentials are reused, cached, weak, or poorly governed. Check Point Research disclosed three vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing implementations, including CVE-2025-67644, a SQLite checkpointer SQL injection, and CVE-2026-28277, an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue. The research showed that SQL injection could be chained with unsafe deserialization to achieve remote code execution where applications expose getstatehistory() with attacker-controlled filters; fixes are available in updated LangGraph and checkpoint packages. CISA announced Binding Operational Directive 26-04, requiring U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to remediate high-risk vulnerabilities under accelerated timelines, with the shortest deadline set at three days. Prioritization is based on factors including public exposure, Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog status, automation potential, and whether exploitation grants partial or full system control. The broader vulnerability management discussion reflected shrinking exploit windows. The Hacker News cited reporting that AI-assisted discovery has increased vulnerability volume and reduced average time-to-exploit, while Aikido’s Docker Hardened Images integration illustrated parallel efforts to reduce container vulnerability noise through signed SBOMs and Docker VEX attestations. IRONSCALES analyzed an image-only callback phishing email posing as a Geek Squad renewal invoice for $270. The message used an empty subject line, a JPEG attachment, and a callback number, with OCR revealing a brand mismatch between Geek Squad and “Windows Defender” copyright text. Industrial security and industry community activity also featured in the window. Radiflow and Barrier announced an OT security networking dinner in Aberdeen focused on cyber resilience, industrial threat visibility, and critical infrastructure security, while The Hacker News published the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners across 95 subcategories.

item.title: Ivanti CVE-2026-10520 Exploitation, ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, and npm Supply Chain Controls
item.description: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability updates affecting BitLocker recovery behavior and continued attention to operational technology visibility gaps. Attackers are reportedly targeting CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw in Ivanti Sentry that enables code execution with root privileges on Internet-exposed secure mobile gateways. Ivanti patched the vulnerability in Sentry versions R10.5.2, R10.6.2, and R10.7.1, but Shadowserver reported soon after that exposed vulnerable systems had already been backdoored in some cases. Shadowserver observed 19 vulnerable Ivanti Sentry instances during scanning and said at least two were backdoored, warning that unpatched exposed systems were likely compromised. The report adds to a broader Ivanti risk pattern: CISA has flagged 34 vulnerabilities across Ivanti products as actively exploited in the wild, including 12 that were also targeted in ransomware attacks. The University of Nottingham confirmed unauthorized access to its student records system, affecting current students and alumni. Have I Been Pwned reported that 454,600 individuals were impacted, with exposed data including email addresses, names, addresses, phone numbers, ethnicities, disabilities, passport numbers, academic enrolment information, and fee payment information. The ShinyHunters extortion gang claimed responsibility and said it stole more than 40GB of documents from the University of Nottingham and its Malaysia and China campuses. The university said it is working with the third party that maintains the affected platform on a forensic investigation and has reported the incident to Action Fraud and the UK Information Commissioner’s Office. BleepingComputer linked the intrusion to a wider ShinyHunters campaign targeting cloud and on-premises Oracle PeopleSoft instances at more than 100 organizations globally. The incident highlights continued actor interest in enterprise student information systems and third-party-maintained platforms containing high-volume personal and administrative records. GitHub announced breaking changes planned for npm version 12 that will disable dependency install scripts by default. The change targets a long-standing supply chain risk in which npm install can execute preinstall, install, and postinstall scripts from transitive dependencies, allowing a compromised package anywhere in the dependency tree to run arbitrary code on developer systems or CI runners. Under the npm 12 model, dependency install scripts will not execute unless explicitly approved at the project level. npm install will also stop resolving Git dependencies or dependencies from remote URLs unless those behaviors are explicitly allowed with relevant options, narrowing default execution and resolution pathways commonly abused in dependency-chain compromise scenarios. Microsoft also resolved a Windows platform issue that caused some Windows Server 2025 systems to boot into BitLocker recovery after the April 2026 security update. The bug affected devices with BitLocker enabled on the OS drive and a specific unrecommended Group Policy configuration involving TPM platform validation and PCR7; fixes were released in KB5094125 for Windows Server 2025 and KB5093998 for Windows 11 23H2. A webinar titled “Closing the OT Security Visibility Gap: From Blind Spots to Continuous Protection” focuses on persistent visibility limitations in operational technology environments. The framing reflects a broader security concern around insufficient asset, traffic, and behavioral visibility across industrial networks where continuous monitoring remains harder than in conventional IT environments. The topic aligns with ongoing pressure on defenders to understand OT exposure and detect changes in environments that often include legacy systems, limited instrumentation, and operational constraints. While the article is webinar-focused rather than incident-driven, it reflects continued industry emphasis on closing monitoring gaps in critical infrastructure and industrial settings.
